coredata.dc:title: Tuning HOMO and LUMO of three region (UV, Vis and IR) photoluminescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for photodegradation of methylene blue
coredata.dc:creator: Dejpasand, Mohamad Taghi
Saievar-Iranizad, Esmaiel
Bayat, Amir
Montaghemi, Aref
Ardekani, Saeed Rahemi
coredata.dc:description: Low cost, gram scale and highly N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with a broad absorption spectrum was synthesized using hydrothermal method by citric acid and urea. The obtained NGQDs possess wide absorption spectrum from ultraviolet to visible and IR range of wavelength. Optical characterization revealed that NGQDs are highly photoluminescent in three region of light (i.e. UV, Vis and IR). The average thickness is lower than 1 nm, meaning that the NGQDs are single, two or three layers of graphene. Lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the NGQDs determined using a cyclic voltammetry (CV) method, and energy band gap was obtained ∼ 1.91 eV. A mechanism of charge carrier generation, separation, transport and photodegradation of methylene blue under illumination was proposed using the energy states. Finally the effect of N concentration on the band gap of NGQDs was simulated and discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NGQD
LUMO
HOMO
Photodegradation
Cyclic voltammetry
Quantum ESPRESSO

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and chemical composition of NGQDs
Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy states
Computational analysis of energy band gap

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the hydrothermal synthesis of NGQDs.
(a) TEM image, (b) EDX, (c) AFM image and (d) Height profile of NGQDs.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman shift spectrum of NGQDs.
Absorbance spectrum of samples with different concentration of Urea (0.35, 0.30, 0.25 and 0.20 g).
(a) Absorbance spectrum and (b-d) The Pl emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths for NGQDs.
UV–vis spectra of NGQDs solution under visible light irradiation.

(a) Calculation of NGQDs band gap with determination of the HOMO and LUMO energy levels, (b) The down converge effect of NGQDs under green laser (532 nm), (c) The photocatalytic mechanism of NGQDs (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(a – h) The structure and DOS of NGQDs.

head.ce:title: Tuning HOMO and LUMO of three region (UV, Vis and IR) photoluminescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for photodegradation of methylene blue

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of basic science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low cost, gram scale and highly N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with a broad absorption spectrum was synthesized using hydrothermal method by citric acid and urea. The obtained NGQDs possess wide absorption spectrum from ultraviolet to visible and IR range of wavelength. Optical characterization revealed that NGQDs are highly photoluminescent in three region of light (i.e. UV, Vis and IR). The average thickness is lower than 1 nm, meaning that the NGQDs are single, two or three layers of graphene. Lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the NGQDs determined using a cyclic voltammetry (CV) method, and energy band gap was obtained ∼ 1.91 eV. A mechanism of charge carrier generation, separation, transport and photodegradation of methylene blue under illumination was proposed using the energy states. Finally the effect of N concentration on the band gap of NGQDs was simulated and discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon based nanomaterials have found great attention due to their low toxicity, high biocompatibility, physical and chemical stability, easy preparation and low cost [1–4]. Among these nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one of the zero-dimensional carbon families are attracting more attention due to their application in various fields such as biosensing, cellular imaging, drug delivery, solar cells, water splitting and wastewater treatment [5–9]. Because of their weak absorption spectrum in the visible region of light, GQDs are not a good choice for photocatalytic degradation [10–13]. To effectively tune the absorption region of GQDs, a promising method is to doping GQDs with heteroatoms such as fluorine, nitrogen and sulfur [14–21].

Doped GQDs have been considerably fabricated for sensing and catalytic application and selection of doping elements depends on the structure and size of heteroatoms [22–24]. Nowadays NGQDs are prepared by both top-down and bottom-up methods, however top-down methods always suffer from some disadvantages, such as need to expensive equipment, low quantum yield and difficult condition to control the size of dots. Bottom-up methods lead to controllable condition over morphology and the size distribution [25–28]. Among bottom-up methods such as solvothermal [29,30], hydrothermal [31–33], pyrolysis [34–37] and electrochemical synthesis [38–40]. hydrothermal is a very simple, cheap and exact method that we report to synthesis highly N doped luminescent GQDs. Nitrogen elements can substitute with carbons in the middle or on the edges of structure as graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N and modify the optical properties of GQDs by changing the band gap edges or creating new levels of energy between them [41,42]. This process helps to optimize the place of luminescence peak and the amount of emitted photons for specific applications. Various researchers have investigated and synthesized NGQDs for photodegradation applications, whereas there are very rare studies on the role of LUMO and HOMO energy levels positions on the photodegradation.

Herein we have used highly amount nitrogen doping to extend the absorption spectrum on the whole of light region (UV, Vis and IR) for photocatalytic applications. Also we calculated the density of states of a simple structure of NGQD to find the effect of N concentration on the band gap and absorption spectrum. To explain the reaction for photodegradation of methylene blue, lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular-orbital (HOMO) states of the NGQDs were determined. Explaining the mechanism of photodegradation using NGQDs under illumination could be informative to promote their utilization in solar application.

For hydrothermal synthesis of NGQDs, 0.30 g citric acid and 0.35 g urea (Sigma-Aldrich) were stirred in 25 cc deionized water for 2 min in room temperature. The solution was putted in 40 cc autoclave and was heated in 160 °C for 4 h. After synthesis process the colorless solution was changed to orange color. Schematic illustration for formation of NGQDs is shown in Fig.1 .

Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was performed by a MIRA3 TESCAN instrument. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using a CM30 300 kV instrument. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a Philips Expert instrument with Cu kα radiation. UV–vis spectra were collected by a Perkin-Elmer lambda 25 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission was recorded using a PL spectrometer (Gilden photonics). Raman spectroscopy was performed by Raman spectrometer (Takram P50C0R10) with 532 nm excitation. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) measurements were used with a three electrode system at room temperature, i.e. Ag/AgCl as the reference, a platinum plate as the counter and NGQDs/glassy carbon as the working electrodes. Acetonitrile including 0.1 M Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6) was used as the solution for CV measurements.

The photocatalytic assessment of NGQDs was studied by preparing a mixture containing 0.02 g of the synthesized NGQDs in 50 mL aqueous solution of Methylene Blue (MB, 0.3 mg L−1). The mixture was stirred at room temperature under irradiation of xenon lamp as the visible light source with a 500 W power. The MB concentration in the solution at different time intervals was determined by UV–vis spectroscopy.

All calculations were performed with the plane-wave density functional theory by the Quantum ESPRESSO package [43]. In self consistent functional (SCF) calculation, we have used 5*5*1 k-point grids and the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) developed by Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) [44], used for the exchange-correlation energy functional. For electron wave function, a plane-wave basis set with 40 eV cut-off energy has been used. The unit cells are separated from them replica by 20 ˚A in every direction. With this vacuum separating, a NGQD can be considered isolated. In addition, atomic positions for all structures are relaxed, so the forces on the atoms are about less than 0.03 eV/˚A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical composition of NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Morphology and chemical composition of NGQDs was characterized by TEM images, EDX, Raman and XRD analysis. TEM image of the prepared sample and its histogram is shown in Fig. 2 -a. Homogeneous and well dispersed distribution of particles is completely obvious in the TEM image, also the histogram shows the particle size is in the range of 4−7 nm and the average size of particles is about 5 nm.

The elemental composition of prepared NGQDs was analyzed by EDX as is shown in Fig. 2-b. The amount of doped Nitrogen is high enough to have an obvious effect on optical properties without disturbing the structure. Weight percent of elements indicates 27 % nitrogen is successfully doped in sample that confirms the synthesis of highly nitrogen doped Graphene quantum dots as it was expected.

To estimate thickness of the as-formed NGQDs, AFM analysis was used as shown in Fig. 2-c. For this purpose, NGQDs solution was drop casted on a mica substrate. Height profile of the NGQDs on the mica in Fig. 2-d, indicates that the thickness of particles is very small ranging from 0.5 to 2.0 nm, meaning that the NGQDs are single, two or three layers of graphene, in a good agreement with TEM image.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the prepared sample is shown in Fig. 3 -a. The NGQDs shows a broad diffraction peak at 26.6 ̊.In comparison with other reports, the peak indicates increasing about 1 ̊ shift, due to lower interlayer space because of higher amount of nitrogen replaced by oxygen in the structure. Raman spectrum of NGQDs is shown in Fig. 3-b. The peaks centered at 1350 and 1577 cm−1 related to the D and G bands of sample, respectively and also the ID/IG ratio is 0.82.

The optical properties were investigated by photoluminescence and UV–vis spectra. In this paper, our goal was to achieve higher absorption at higher wavelengths in the graphene quantum dot structure, which we tested with nitrogen doping. According to the Fig. 4 , between the samples with different precursor concentrations, the sample with the highest nitrogen concentration had a higher peak intensity in the visible region, higher concentrations (more than 0.35 g urea) was analyzed but disrupted the composition of the substance and led to formation of particles that weren’t quantum dot. It was important for us to be able to have a higher absorption in the visible area so that for optical sensors we can do excitation with less energy photons and also in photocatalytic applications, visible light could be used as much as possible. Therefore, the sample that had the most absorption in the visible area was selected as the optimal sample and other analyzes were performed on this sample.

Fig. 5 -a shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the NGQDs solution. The peak at 255 nm refers to sp2 hybridized structure resulted by CC and CC and the peak at 381 is related to sp3 hybridized structure resulted by the CO bond due to the n-π* transition [45,46]. The appeared new peak at 640 nm and also NIR region absorption are attributed to the formation of partial conjugated π electrons in the NGQDs with high nitrogen doping. An average size of the NGQDs about 5 nm is enough to make an extensive partial conjugated π electrons system for a NIR band absorption [47]. The relation between π electron and large macromolecular domains with the NIR absorption mechanism observed in graphene oxide (GO) [48,49]. These peaks could result in a higher rate of photocatalytic activity of NGQDs.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra indicate excitation-dependent PL emission. At low wavelength excitations, two peaks appear at 420 and 460 nm that corresponds to sp2 and sp3 peaks in UV–vis spectra. The localized π electrons in carbon based double bonds (mainly CC), lead to UV absorption and consequently results in UV and visible PL emission due to the vibration relaxation and inter-band transition of excited electrons. Visible emission is because of the fact that the radiative recombination happens after inter-band transition, then UV absorption converts to visible light emission (Fig. 5-b). Furthermore, another peak between 600–700 nm and the last one at ∼ 890 nm attributed to N doping that created new energy levels between the HOMO and LUMO of NGQDs (Fig. 5-c and d). Generally, the UV and visible absorption in the structure of the NGQDs, results in related visible PL emission after vibration relaxation and the NIR PL emissions are relevant to the new created π bonds between C and N. The π electrons will need lower energy to excite. The concentration of N also will determine the emission intensity in this region. This process will be discussed in computational analysis of energy band gap. In the band structure view, presence of nitrogen leads to creating new energy states for excitons. These new energy states can change the probability of electron-hole recombination. Therefore emitted photons will cover a wider range of wavelengths.

Moreover, the quantum yield of NGQDs was determined by using quinine sulfate as a reference (shown in Table 1 ) [50]. It was calculated according to
(1) φ= φr (I/I r)(n2/n r 2)(A r/A)
where φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index (1.33 for water), and A is the optical density. The subscript “r” refers to the reference of known quantum yield. The PL quantum yield measured using quinine sulfate as a reference is 9 %.

We analyzed the photocatalytic performance of NGQDs for degrading methylene blue as an organic dye. The UV–vis spectra of the degraded MB solutions at different times are shown in Fig. 6 . It is seen that the intensity of the characteristic absorbance peak of MB at λ =670 nm decreases versus irradiation time, which indicates the degradation of MB by the synthesized NGQDs. We calculated the degradation percent of MB using the following equation [51]:
(2)
Degradation% =
C 0
-
C t
C 0
× 100
Where C0 and Ct are the concentration of MB at time 0 and t, respectively. As high as 98 % of MB dye was degraded after 150 min visible light irradiation by the NGQDs.

The high degradation percent of MB with visible light is related to wide absorbance, presence of a new PL peak in visible range and convenient position of HOMO and LUMO energy level of NGQDs. The doping of N, increases the energy levels and creates delocalized states by formation of new bonds with carbons. To explain the role of NGQDs on the photodegradation of MB, HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the NGQDs was measured using CV technique.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy states
ce:section.ce:para:
In principle, the band gap of GQDs can be tuned between 0 eV (graphene) and ∼ 6 eV (benzene) by changing size, shape and functional groups and these parameters depend on the synthesis method. For determination of LUMO and HOMO energy levels of the NGQDs quantum dots, CV technique was utilized. Applying potentials above the LUMO or below the HOMO versus vacuum (or a well-known reference such as Ag/AgCl), leads to the cathodic and anodic currents, respectively, that can be used for determination of LUMO and HOMO. To prepare the electrode for CV measurements, a drop containing 5 μl NGQDs and 0.5 μl nafion (5% W) was drop casted on a polished glassy carbon and dried naturally at room temperature. Measured cathodic and anodic currents in presence and absence of NGQDs on glassy carbon is shown in Fig. 7 -a. The oxidation and reduction peaks related to electron and hole injections were used for determination of the EHOMO and ELUMO using the following three equations [32]
(3) EHOMO = − e (EOx, vs. NHE+4.75) (eV)
(4) ELUMO = − e (ERed, vs. NHE+4.75) (eV)
(5) ENHE=EAg/AgCl+0.197

As can be seen in Fig. 7-a, the working electrode indicates a reduction peak at -0.26 V and an oxidation peak at 1.65 V versus Ag/AgCl as a well-known reference. The ELUMO and EHOMO were calculated ELUMO= -4.69 eV and EHOMO= -6.6 eV versus vacuum, respectively. The difference between ELUMO and EHOMO is the band gap energy and was calculated 1.91 eV using these equations. The down-converge effect of NGQDs is shown in Fig. 7-b using a green laser (532 nm).

The photocatalytic mechanism of NGQDs is shown in Fig. 7-c. The NGQDs with a large surface area is able to attach MB molecules. Therefore more MB dye could interact with the photocatalyst compound, results in high photodegradation efficiency. By the light irradiation, the photo-excited electrons move from LUMO band of MB towards the LUMO band of NGQDs and the holes move in opposite direction, this process will degrade MB by trapping electrons from its structure. In the other way, the electrons react with the dissolved oxygen and produce reactive superoxide radicals and the holes are trapped by the hydroxyl ions at the surface of the photocatalyst to generate hydroxyl radicals (OH˚). The produced active radicals react with MB adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst and degrade the dye molecules. The new levels created by nitrogen make the band gap smaller and help electrons with less energy to participate in this process so leads to creating more holes that can trap electrons of MB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational analysis of energy band gap
ce:section.ce:para: As it was difficult to control every parameters such as size and height before and after N-doping by different concentration, after synthesis the NGQDs with maximum N, The DOS of GQD and N-GQDs with the same size and one layer (to eliminate the size and height effect) was calculated to find the impact of N concentration on the band gap energy and absorption spectrum.

Fig. 8 shows the structures in the left column and their corresponding DOS in the right one. The structure of graphene quantum dot and its DOS is indicated in Fig. 8a and b. According to these figures, distribution of px, py, and pz orbitals shows the role of pz on the amount of band gap energy that is 2.88 eV. Fig. 8c and d show the structure and DOS of N-GQD with one N atom to analyze the existence effect of N on the density of states and band gap energy of GQD structure. The DOS of N-GQD proves that with doping N in the structure, the band gap energy becomes smaller in comparison with GQD. Due to the hybridization of the pz orbitals of N and C, Л bonds are created in N-GQD that caused to pz orbitals energy window becomes wider, and the band gap energy reduces to 2.63 eV. Also the peak shift of pz orbitals of C to more positive energies in comparison with GQD is due to broken of CC bond and creating a Л bond between C and N. In Fig. 8e the structure of N-GQD with different kinds of N (pyrrolic, pyridinic and graphitic) is illustrated, its DOS in Fig. 8f shows that by increasing the share of N in structure, the band gap energy will reduce to 2.24 eV. The Fig. 8f also shows that near the band gap, the N3 has the main role in impacting on carbon's orbitals because of existing a double bond between N and C in CN bond. This double bond leads to formation of a Л bond that has lower energy than σ. For the final step, the OH was added to calculated structure in Fig.8g to getting closer to the experimental situation. It is obvious from Fig.4h that by adding OH the band gap energy reduced again. The band gap energy for the last structure is 1.78 eV. Comparison between GQD and N-GQDs shows the effect of N doping on reducing the bang gap energy and consequently red shift of absorption peaks that confirms the relation between high concentration of N and red shift of absorption peaks in experimental analysis section.

Low cost, gram scale and highly N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with a broad absorption spectrum was obtained using hydrothermal method by citric acid and urea as the precursors. Cyclic voltammetry measurements were utilized to directly determine the LUMO and HOMO energy levels. The broadband emission of the synthesized N-GQDs could be used in a wide range of applications like photocatalysts, solar cells and light emitting devices. For instance the photocatalytic performance of NGQDs was analyzed for degradation of MB as an organic dye, and about 98 % of MB was degraded after 150 min visible light irradiation. The high degradation percent of MB with visible light is related to wide absorbance, presence of a new PL peak in visible range and convenient position of HOMO and LUMO energy level of NGQDs.

Mohamad Taghi Dejpasand: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Esmaiel Saievar-Iranizad: Supervision. Amir Bayat: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Aref Montaghemi: Formal analysis. Saeed Rahemi Ardekani: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Research Council of the Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports.
